content:
For a decade, the disruption narrative in financial services pointed outward. Crypto-native protocols would disintermediate banks. DeFi would replace prime brokers. Stablecoins would end correspondent banking. The threat came from outside the industry, from systems that didn’t need banks at all.
That narrative was wrong — not because the technology was wrong, but because it misidentified the vector.
The institutions most threatened by tokenization are not threatened by crypto. They are threatened by other banks.
Why the Crypto Disruption Story Didn’t Land The premise of crypto-as-disruptor required something that hasn’t happened: institutional trust at scale without a regulated wrapper.
DeFi protocols are extraordinarily efficient at certain things — permissionless liquidity, programmable settlement, transparent collateralization. They are extraordinarily bad at other things — KYC, legal recourse, compliance with capital requirements, fiduciary accountability. For retail speculation, those deficiencies are acceptable. For a pension fund settling a $2 billion position, they are not.
Crypto-native finance and regulated finance are not competing for the same customer at the same moment. The disruption thesis assumed convergence that hasn’t come, and may not come in any near-term timeframe that matters for strategic planning.
What has come, quietly and without much fanfare, is tokenization inside regulated institutions. And that changes everything.
Tokenization Is Not a Product. It Is Infrastructure. This distinction is where most strategic analysis fails.
When BlackRock launched its BUIDL fund — a tokenized money market fund now holding approximately $1.8 billion in assets — the story was framed as a product launch. A new way for institutions to access short-term yield. That framing is too narrow.
BUIDL is infrastructure. It demonstrated that a regulated, institutional-grade financial product could be represented on a blockchain, with 24/7 settlement, programmable transfer restrictions, and automated yield distribution — without sacrificing any of the compliance properties that institutional investors require. The product was the proof of concept. The infrastructure is the point.
The same logic applies to JPMorgan’s Onyx, to tokenized repo markets settling in minutes instead of days, to the wave of tokenized treasury products now processing billions in weekly volume. These are not experiments. They are the early operational layer of a different kind of financial system — one built on the same institutions, the same regulatory frameworks, the same fiduciary structures, but with a radically different cost basis and product surface area.
What Banks That Tokenize Actually Gain Settlement latency is the obvious one. T+2 to T+0, or near-zero, meaningfully changes capital efficiency for every institution on both sides of a trade. The less obvious gains are more consequential.
Programmable money expands the product surface area. A tokenized deposit is not just a faster deposit. It is a deposit that can carry conditions — release on delivery of an asset, automatic compliance checks at transfer, yield that compounds and distributes without manual intervention. Every financial product built on programmable money can do things that paper-based contracts and batch-settlement systems cannot.
Composability creates distribution without intermediaries. A bank that tokenizes its credit products can make them available across any platform that speaks the same protocol. This isn’t disintermediation — it is distribution at zero marginal cost. The bank retains the credit relationship, the regulatory standing, the customer. It gains reach it would otherwise have to build or buy.
CBDC interoperability is not optional. In Brazil, Real Digital — the central bank’s digital currency — operates on a DLT infrastructure. The institutions that integrate with that infrastructure early gain settlement access, product integration possibilities, and operational familiarity that latecomers will have to buy at a premium. Every major CBDC program creates the same dynamic. Banks that tokenize are already speaking the language. Banks that didn’t will need a translator.
The Actual Threat Model Incumbent banks face a cost structure built for a world that is being systematically re-engineered.
Correspondent banking margins exist because cross-border settlement is slow and opaque. Custodial fees exist because asset transfers require manual reconciliation. Trade finance margins exist because verifying the chain of title on physical goods is expensive and slow. Tokenization does not eliminate these business lines. It eliminates the friction that justifies the margin.
A bank that has tokenized its settlement layer, its deposit base, and its asset portfolio operates at a structurally lower cost than one that hasn’t. It can offer better yield, faster settlement, and richer product features — inside the same regulatory wrapper, with the same deposit insurance, the same legal recourse.
That is not a crypto competitor. That is a bank. And it is the most dangerous competitor an incumbent financial institution can face.
The Window Is Shorter Than It Looks Infrastructure transitions in financial services appear slow and then become sudden. The institutions that moved early on real-time payment rails didn’t look visionary at the time — they looked like they were spending money on plumbing. Until they weren’t.
Tokenization is at the same inflection point. The experimentation phase is ending. The operational phase has begun. BlackRock, Franklin Templeton, JPMorgan, and a growing list of institutions are not experimenting. They are settling real money on tokenized infrastructure at scale.
The question for every bank that hasn’t moved is not whether to tokenize. It is whether the decision to wait was made deliberately, with a clear-eyed view of the timeline — or by default, because it was easy to defer.
Default decisions in infrastructure are expensive. In financial services, they are occasionally existential.
Bruno Grossi leads Digital Assets & Emerging Technologies at Inter, Brazil’s largest digital bank by customer count (NASDAQ: INTR). He writes on digital money infrastructure, CBDC architecture, and the strategic implications of tokenization for regulated financial institutions.
summary:
For a decade, the disruption narrative in financial services pointed outward. Crypto-native protocols would disintermediate banks. DeFi would replace prime brokers. Stablecoins would end correspondent banking. The threat came from outside the industry, from systems that didn’t need banks at all.

That narrative was wrong — not because the technology was wrong, but because it misidentified the vector.

The institutions most threatened by tokenization are not threatened by crypto. They are threatened by other banks.
title: Banks Won't Be Disrupted by Crypto. They'll Be Disrupted by Banks That Tokenized.

content:
There is a class of attack with an uncomfortable name: harvest now, decrypt later.
The premise is simple. A sophisticated adversary — a nation-state, a well-resourced criminal organization — intercepts and stores encrypted financial data today. They cannot read it. That’s fine. They are patient. They store it and wait for quantum computers capable of breaking current encryption to become available. When that day comes, years or decades from now, they decrypt everything retroactively.
Every transaction. Every counterparty. Every balance.
This is not a theoretical concern. It is an active intelligence strategy. And it is especially dangerous for one specific type of financial infrastructure: central bank digital currencies.
Why CBDCs Are a Unique Target CBDCs are not cryptocurrencies. They are sovereign money — a direct digital liability of the central bank, with legal tender status. When Brazil’s Real Digital processes a transaction, that record carries a different weight than a DeFi swap on a public blockchain.
That weight makes it a target.
The cryptographic schemes protecting most CBDC infrastructure today — RSA, ECDSA, the elliptic curve signatures used in virtually every blockchain-derived system — are vulnerable to Shor’s algorithm, a quantum computation method capable of factoring large integers exponentially faster than any classical computer. The moment a sufficiently powerful quantum computer exists, these schemes break.
The financial community generally treats this as a distant problem. It is not.
NIST spent eight years evaluating post-quantum cryptographic algorithms. In 2024, they finalized three standards: ML-KEM (for key encapsulation), ML-DSA and SLH-DSA (for digital signatures). These algorithms are resistant to both classical and quantum attacks. They exist. They are standardized. The engineering to deploy them is non-trivial but tractable.
The question is not whether to migrate. It is when — and whether the systems being built today are designed to make that migration possible.
What the Research Found In July 2024, I co-authored an analysis published in the Cryptology ePrint Archive examining exactly this question in the context of DLT-based CBDC infrastructure: can post-quantum algorithms be applied to the signing and verification operations that underpin a CBDC system, and at what cost?
The findings were instructive — and in some cases, uncomfortable.
The good news: post-quantum signature schemes can be integrated into DLT-based CBDC infrastructure. The algorithms are mature enough for production consideration. The feasibility is real.
The harder news: the performance profile is different. Post-quantum signatures are significantly larger than their classical equivalents. ML-DSA signatures are roughly 10–50× larger than ECDSA, depending on the security level chosen. SLH-DSA is even larger, though more conservative. On a high-throughput payment rail — the kind a central bank digital currency needs — this has implications for network bandwidth, storage, and transaction validation latency.
These are solvable engineering problems. But they require design choices made now, not during a post-quantum migration panic in 2031.
The Actual Risk Model Here is what most CBDC programs are not saying publicly: the threat window for harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks is already open.
Transactions processed on Real Digital today will exist as permanent cryptographic records. If those records are stored — by any sufficiently motivated party — and if RSA or ECDSA is the underlying signature scheme, then the confidentiality of those transactions is contingent on quantum computers never reaching cryptographic relevance.
That is a bet. It may be a reasonable bet for the next five years. It is a much less reasonable bet for the next twenty.
The systems being architected today will likely still be processing transactions in 2040. The cryptographic assumptions built into them will be load-bearing for that entire period. Getting them wrong is not an upgrade problem — it is a redesign problem.
What Responsible Design Looks Like Cryptographic agility — the ability to swap signature schemes without rebuilding the underlying infrastructure — is the architectural decision that separates CBDCs built to last from CBDCs built to ship.
It means abstracting the signature layer. It means not hardcoding ECDSA into the consensus protocol. It means having a migration path that doesn’t require reissuing every credential in the system.
It is not glamorous engineering. It rarely makes the press release. But it is the difference between a financial system that can adapt to a post-quantum world and one that becomes a liability the moment the threat materializes.
Brazil’s Real Digital has the opportunity to get this right — to become not just a technically functional CBDC, but a reference implementation for post-quantum-ready sovereign digital money.
That window is open. It does not stay open indefinitely.
Bruno Grossi leads Digital Assets & Emerging Technologies at Inter. He co-authored Applying Post-Quantum Cryptography Algorithms to a DLT-Based CBDC Infrastructure, published in the Cryptology ePrint Archive in July 2024.
summary:
There is a class of attack with an uncomfortable name: harvest now, decrypt later.

The premise is simple. A sophisticated adversary — a nation-state, a well-resourced criminal organization — intercepts and stores encrypted financial data today. They cannot read it. That’s fine. They are patient. They store it and wait for quantum computers capable of breaking current encryption to become available. When that day comes, years or decades from now, they decrypt everything retroactively.
title: The Clock Problem: Why Every CBDC Being Built Today Has a Security Expiration Date

Inter Head of Digital Assets & Emerging Technologies Belo Horizonte, Brazil · 2020 –
Leading the bank’s digital asset infrastructure, CBDC strategy, and emerging technology initiatives at Brazil’s most prominent digital bank (NASDAQ: INTR, 30M+ customers).
Research Applying Post-Quantum Cryptography Algorithms to a DLT-Based CBDC Infrastructure: Comparative and Feasibility Analysis Cryptology ePrint Archive · July 2024
Comparative and feasibility analysis of post-quantum cryptographic algorithms applied to DLT-based CBDC systems. Among the first published work addressing this challenge in the context of Brazil’s Real Digital program.
O que não é o Real Digital: desvendando mitos sobre a CBDC Brasileira August 2023
Myth-busting analysis of Brazil’s central bank digital currency — what Real Digital actually is, and what it is not.
